source.title: Executive Functions Can Be Improved in Preschoolers Through Systematic Playing in Educational Settings: Evidence From a Longitudinal Study

confidence_rationale: Longitudinal study (3 timepoints: baseline, post-intervention, 8-month follow-up) testing 32 specifically designed games targeting inhibitory control, working memory, and cognitive flexibility. Key finding -- sustained EF improvements at 8 months -- is relevant to DEV-NEED-013 (games-with-rules training). Source quality is moderate (Frontiers in Psychology). Mapping fidelity is moderate: the designed games include rule-based structures but are not specifically games-with-rules in the Piagetian sense. Transfer risk is low.
failure_signatures: Games were adult-designed and administered (60 daily sessions), not naturalistic peer games -- social coordination and bilateral frame-maintenance are not being trained.
Effect sizes between T1 and T2 (immediately post-intervention) were not significant; only T1-T3 (8-month) effects reached significance -- medium-term consolidation rather than immediate transfer.
No active control matched for adult game-facilitation -- cannot rule out general engagement effects.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Systematic playing of 32 purpose-designed rule-based games (60 sessions, 45 min/day) produced significant, sustained improvements in EF at 8-month follow-up in preschoolers (ages 4-6). EF gains were not significant immediately post-intervention but emerged over 8 months.
mapping.ree_translation: Supports DEV-NEED-013: structured rule-based play can be designed to train EF components. The delayed emergence of effects (T1-T3 but not T1-T2) is particularly interesting for REE: it suggests consolidation processes operate after the play intervention, consistent with MECH-197's claim that each play type trains subsystems that the next type builds on. The pattern of delayed gains may indicate the same sleep-mediated consolidation that REE's sleep architecture (MECH-272, MECH-273) should produce.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Games are purpose-designed by researchers, not organically arising peer games. The social coordination dimension (bilateral frame maintenance, partner monitoring) is absent. Cannot speak to ARC-049/INV-059 or Q-035. Maps primarily to DEV-NEED-013 as a training existence proof.
mapping.source_context: Preschool children ages 4-6, 60-session intervention, longitudinal design with 8-month follow-up, Chile.

confidence_components.notes: Frontiers in Psychology is reputable but lower-impact than flagship journals. Longitudinal design is a strength. Mapping fidelity limited by adult-designed game context.
